text: The video shows the initial setup for a robotic abdominal perineal resection, with access to the peritoneal cavity established and multiple robotic trocars inserted into the abdominal wall.
The video shows radiation-related changes in the upper rectum and sigmoid colon, with the terminal ileum visibly adhered to the right pelvic side wall.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to carefully lyse adhesions or attachments in the pelvic area, taking great care to avoid injury to the small intestine.
The surgeon uses a third robotic arm to facilitate the rectal dissection, having freed the small bowel from pelvic adhesions to allow further progress in the operation.

The surgeon has moved the small bowel out of the pelvis and incised the peritoneum along the right lateral edge of the sigmoid and rectum. A plane is developed between the mesorectum and retroperitoneal structures, with visible challenges due to radiation-induced adhesions.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery to carefully lyse dense adhesions in the pelvic region, with visible attention to anatomical planes. A lighted ureteral stent is observed, although it is malpositioned and located only in the distal ureter.

The surgeon continues dissection to open the holy plane, encountering slow progress. Attention shifts to the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon, which are precisely dissected using a hook cautery. Gas visibly enters the space behind the peritoneum, aiding in the expansion of this area.

The surgeon uses the hook cautery to perform a precise layer-by-layer dissection, carefully maneuvering between medial and lateral views to avoid damaging structures in the fibrotic post-radiation tissues.

The surgeon works along the left side of the proximal rectum, addressing remaining attachments between the mesosigmoid and retroperitoneal structures. The pedicle is skeletonized and taken with a stapler due to its thickness, ensuring good hemostasis.

The surgeon lyses adhesions tethering the right lateral aspect of the colon using a hook cautery in a layer-by-layer fashion. Traction and counter-traction are applied to reveal normal anatomic planes, with the tumor located significantly lower in the pelvis, approximately 2.5 centimeters from the anal verge.

The surgeon uses a third arm to wrap around and lift the colon, providing anterior traction on the rectum to facilitate exposure in the pelvis. This maneuver allows for opening the posterior anatomic planes and progressing circumferentially around the rectum. Additional instruments are used to dissect posteriorly, laterally, and anteriorly, with challenges noted in suctioning within the male pelvis.

The surgeon places a ratech into the pelvis to absorb remaining fluid, while using a hook cautery to achieve hemostasis by following normal anatomic planes. An air seal device is employed through an eight millimeter port in the right upper quadrant to clear smoke from the surgical field.

The surgeon applies lateral tension toward the right lateral stalk, carefully developing the area by establishing traction and counter traction to follow the normal anatomic planes. Similarly, traction is applied to open the left lateral stalk and its attachments to the rectum.

The surgeon focuses on anterior dissection of the mesorectum, using a robotic arm to provide counter-traction by pulling the prostate anteriorly. The narrow male pelvis presents challenges, but the setup of robotic trocars aids in avoiding arm collisions during the dissection.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection in a to-and-fro manner, progressively reaching down to the pelvic floor. The robotic arm provides additional counter traction, facilitating dissection in the narrow pelvic space.

The surgeon identifies the seminal vesicle and vas deferens during anterior dissection. Dissection continues along the right side, revealing muscle fibers of the levator muscles. The pelvic floor is reached, and further posterior dissection is performed to this level.

The surgeon identifies the ureter within the retroperitoneum, using Firefly imaging to plan the colonic transection. A stapling device is utilized for the transection, and a colostomy is brought out through a trocar site in the left lower quadrant. Lateral attachments are released to gain additional length for the colostomy.
